title: Theater Arts Program Review 2019-2020 (Part 1)
text:
Theater Arts Comprehensive Program Review for AY 2019-2020
1
Section 1: Description of Program
Mission
Over the past few years, the Theater program’s mission has expanded to not only providing an intensive
and comprehensive collegiate performing arts experience for our traditional four-year college bound
students but to actively engage with our community as well. With this in mind, and having our full-time
instructional support positions in place, we have intentionally increased our number of productions to
both meet our degree requirements for of our traditional students as well as to provide more
opportunities for our community to engage with the Theater program, and the Institution in general. In
addition, the Theater program continues to collaborate with the Music program in an effort to foster a
multi-disciplinary approach to our subject matter which helps us better serve the diverse needs of all of
our students.
Staffing and Structure
The Theater program is staffed with two full-time instructional support specialists and a full-time
Theater instructor. While we are a small college theater program with small budgets and limited
resources, compared to similliar sized colleges in larger districts, we manage to produce high quality
shows serving both our students and the community. Our staff and faculty have broad skill-sets resulting
from diverse backgrounds in the arts, both in the professional and the academic worlds, which allow us
to be very nimble as we often have to share and change roles through out the academic year.
Primary Purpose
The Theater program’s primary purpose is to serve our traditional, four-year university bound students
by offering courses which lead to three degrees and one certificate;
Associate in Arts Degree in Theater Performance (AA)
Associate in Arts Degree in Theatre Arts for Transfer (AA-T)
Associate in Arts Degree in Musical Theatre (AA)
Certificate of Achievement in Technical Theatre
All of our courses except for non credit course are transferrable and degree applicable. We also serve
our non-traditional students and community members with a versatile non-credit course where they can
participate in productions and hone their skills while working in a professional and collaborative
atmosphere. We are a production based program, meaning that much of what we teach is taught through
the process of producing live theatrical events which is then supported by study in the classroom. For the
past 2 years we have produced at least 4 plays a year;
Fall musical, where both we have both traditional students with some non-traditional students (a
mix of K-12 studets and community members).
Wintercession show, for the past 2 years they were original plays written by local playwrights,
mostly comprised of non-traditional students.
Spring production, mostly comprised of traditional students.
Musical Theater summer camp, mostly comprised of K-12 students.

-- 1 of 11 --

title: Theater Arts Program Review 2019-2020 (Part 2)
text:
Theater Arts Comprehensive Program Review for AY 2019-2020
2
We also provide instruction with the Cooperative Work Experience Education (CWEE) for academic
individualized work-based learning opportunties where students get a chance to work in a real world
setting right here at the Kenneth Ford Theater.
Section 2: External Factors with Significant Impact on the Program
Production based College Theater programs require a work force with very specific skill sets, such as;
production managers, technical directors, designers (costume, set, lights, etc), stage directors, and many
more (too many to list here). They then must work together in a highly prescribed and efficient manner,
which requires expertise in specific areas and appropriate numbers in the work force, to meet the
demands of the production schedule.
The reality is that we are a small College Theater program with relatively small budgets, limited
resources, and a bare bones staff compared to larger programs. While, we believe we have reached an
economy of scale as we have begun to produce more shows a year, which we feel better utilizes our
staff, faculty, and existing budgets, this has lead to burn-out and a loss of effeciency.
A College Theater program requires certain conditions that must be present to be considered viable;
An adherence to a production schedule where rehearsals and technical aspects are brought
together in a precise manner, in a way that reflects the professional and academic world.
Collaboration with qualified instructors that have appropriate training that are in leadership roles,
such as Director, Musical Director, and Designers.
Careful selection of scripts used to create a season of productions that form a meaningful whole
during the student’s tenure in the program.
Efforts to maintain these conditions have led to a real danger of over-stressing the sole full-faculty
member in the theater program. Burnout has proven to be a real issue this year as we have made gains in
general student numbers in courses within the program, FTEs, and theater majors.
While the single full-time faculty member has volunteered to teach in overload since 2011, largely to
insure that students achieve Theater degrees and certificates, this has led to the delay of the creation of
an AA in Technical Theater, and other important curricular development. The full-time faculty member
must serve as stage director, technical director, and set and lighting designer for all productions
pertaining to traditional tranfer level courses. The current faculty member does possess all of the
required skills to perform all of these roles but it’s not possible to perform them all at the same time.
Over the past several years an attempt has been made to hire adjunct instructors to help teach the
required courses for the various theater degreees but this has proven to be ineffective. The region simply
does not have a significant pool of qualified instructors.
Based on this evidence it is strongly recommended that the College of the Siskiyous hire another full-
time theater faculty to teach classes in performance and share the management theater program. Please
see CQIP 1.

-- 2 of 11 --

title: Theater Arts Program Review 2019-2020 (Part 3)
text:
Theater Arts Comprehensive Program Review for AY 2019-2020
3
Section 3: Progress on Outcomes and Assessment
Starting this year (AY 19/20) the Theater program has begun creating Canvas shells for courses taught
in each semester regardless of the modality. The ultimate goal is to have Student Learning Outcomes
linked to both individual assessments and to Program Learning Outcomes for each class. The advantage
of this method is that we will then be able to import data across platforms as a way to report the
outcomes. In the Spring of 2019 we began the process of mapping Program Learning Outcomes to
Student Learning Outcomes for each degree path in the Theater Program.

title: Theater Arts Program Review 2019-2020 (Part 6)
text:
Theater Arts Comprehensive Program Review for AY 2019-2020
8
Section 5: Evaluation and Other Unit-Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research:
In spite of external challenges mentioned in previous chapters the theater program continues to be
successful, certainly in regards to Student numbers and success rates. Annual selection of plays and high
quality productions which are recognized by the community and regional college organizations.
We have had a series of notable productions which have attracted students to us and we continue to
deliver a quality collegiate theater experiences. The result being, several high school programs out of
Siskiyous county have expressed interest in our program.
Much of what we do is due to having a staff that has a nimble and diverse skill set, able to share jobs and
play multiple roles at once and in succesion all the while being passionate and committed to student
success. We all have a Full-Time Instructor who is dynamic and able to serve in many capacities
(program leader, director, tech Director, can teach every class in program catalogue)
We have created strong relationships with local high schools and K-12 regional arts program. This has
increased our student counts and created a reliable recruitment of students. We have good retention
numbers because we have stablished transfer relationship with SOU and have participated KCACTF,
which leads to degree completion.
We have been proactive in taking advantage of opportunities. We have several talented teachers able to
teach at the non credit level so we have begun to create non credit classes and certificates which has led
to a sharp increase in K-12 students and non-traditional students participating with our program, leading
to increases in FTEs.
Section 6: Progress on Prior Goals
Over the past four years the theater program has focused on 3 main areas; defining, maintaining, and
improving the program. We have strived for sustainability and growth as we have had to balance our
limited resources with the growing interest in our department due to our diverse and robust programing.
Defining the Program
College Theater programs all look a little different depending on a multitude of variables, but the more
successful programs have a clearly defined concentration, while still offering strong foundational lower
division courses. In recent years, we have produced a series of popular large cast musical productions
with very high production values, and people of begun to take notice. As a result Theater majors are on
the rise at the college with the musical theater degree still being our most popular degree. While we
benefit from the attention musicals provide they also require a great deal of resources to produce both
financial and human. With the resources in place to produce musicals we have decided to produce more
shows over the course year, which gives us several advantages, such as; using our support staff more
efficiently, integrating class work into our production, and defining us as a viable robust college theater
program.

-- 8 of 11 --

title: Theater Arts Program Review 2019-2020 (Part 7)
text:
Theater Arts Comprehensive Program Review for AY 2019-2020
9
Maintaining the Program
Besides the obvious recruitment advantages of “branding” the program there is also benefit in gaining
effectiveness when you can structure your budget and schedule according to the types (and numbers) of
shows you produce in an academic year. The more we know ourselves the better we can build systems
that work in accordance with our particular classes and productions.
Refine our processes
A theater program handbook is in the process of being developed to establish guidelines and processes
to help ensure the proper functioning of both the Kenneth Ford Theater and the Theater program. We
have always used production calendars and sound budgeting practices during the course of the academic
year but we need to codify our work so that it can be repeatible by others. This document will be
essential as we continue to grow where we will gain more students and potentially more staff and
faculty. We are certainly busier than we ever have been so we have to take steps to make sure that COS
staff, faculty (and students!), community members and many other regional users are all on the same
page.
Increase the number of productions
The Theater program has grown to producing four shows a year, which allows us a great deal of
flexiblity in regards to what we can offer. We have always been committed to producing a Fall musical
and either a comedic or dramatic show in the Spring semester. For the past several years for our third
show, we have offered a month long theater camp during the Summer semester. And finally for our
fourth show, for the past two years we have produced at least one original production and to great
success!
Improve the Program
As we begin to move into producing shows which feature local artists and the creation of devised theater
works we have discovered the need to formalize our relationships with these local individuals and
organizations to help communicate and define our relationship with our local arts community.
For several years we have hosted a summer camp which is in partnership with Mount Shasta Children’s
Theater and because of its popularity and the importance to the Theater program we feel we have had to
formalize this arrangement.
Last, year (AY 18/19) we collaborated with two local artists, Tim Holt and David Theno on two separate
productions. Starting in the summer of 2018 we began rehearsals on Tim’s original script “Walking with
Walt” with performances early in the Fall 2018 semester. In the Winter 2019 intercession the Theater
program produced Dave Theno’s original musical “The Electro-Magnetic Soul Vehicle” to great
community reaction.
In an effort to provide a broader collegiate experience for our students we have begun to reestablish our
relationship with the Kennedy Center American College Theater Festival (KCACTF). Our relationship
with this organization has proven to be very benifical for our students and is a highlight of our year.
Students who participate are expected to audition for scholarships at 4 year schools and for the
opportunity to work at regional professional theaters. This experience also includes seminars,

title: Theater Arts Program Review 2019-2020 (Part 8)
text:
-- 9 of 11 --

Theater Arts Comprehensive Program Review for AY 2019-2020
10
workshops, and a chance to talk to students and professors at other schools giving our students a
wonderful view of what opportunities await them.
Section 7: Four Year-Action Plan and Vision for the Future
1. Hire a 2nd Full time Theater Instructor
COS has had the good fortune of being able to hire a qualified part-time instructor and choreographer
for the past few semesters. Within that time we have demonstrated that the theater program operates the
most effectively and efficiently when there are two faculty members present. Hiring an additional Full
time Theater instructor will provide more stability with an aready known outcome of success.
Please see CQIP 1
1A. Creation of Technical Theater AA and update of Technical Theater Certificate.
After we have restored the 2nd Full-Time Theater Faculty position we can then continue our
efforts to create a Technical Theater degree as well update the Technical Theater Certificate.
2. Increase our Non-Credit course offerings
While we still suffer from a lack of instructors qualified to teach our credit class we do however
currently have several instructors qualified to teach at the non-credit level. Utilizing these valuable
available community assets allows to further engage with community members and K-12 students and
provides for recruitment opportunities.
With expanding numbers of degree seeking students and communtiy members we are able to provide
more opportunities for engage through participating in our Theatrical Productions. Because of this
increase in interest in our program we feel it nessecary to be more specific about the types of
productions and other class offerings our program can provide for its students. The following classes
will be created to satisify this need;
THEA 0XXX Audition Showcase
THEA 0XXX Senior Theatre
THEA 0XXX Summer Theatre Camp
THEA 0XXX One-Act Workshop
THEA 0XXX Technical Experience
3. Create and implement a four year course review cycle for all Theater Classes.
Currently the Theater program is moving most classes to a Hybrid or Online modality, which will allow
us the most flexibility in regards to scheduling so that we may better serve our students. All courses will
have a canvas shell, where all assignments, assessments, and student learning outcomes will be stored.
4. Continue with our efforts to expand our areas of focus and “Brand” the program.
While we have had success with our most popular degree offering, Musical Theater, and continue to
produce Classical, Modern, and Contempory Theater selections so that we may provide the nessecary
rigor and collegiate experience for our students we feel that entering the world of Devised Theater is our

-- 10 of 11 --

title: Theater Arts Program Review 2019-2020 (Part 9)
text:
Theater Arts Comprehensive Program Review for AY 2019-2020
11
next best step in the growth of our program. There are several reasons for this decision; there is a
growing interest in the KCACTF region 7, our faculty and staff can provide expertise in the area, it
allows to produce original productions and interface with our community, and meets the needs of our
students.
5. Create and implement annual theater equipment inspection schedule.
Over the past several years we have made strides to improve the facilites and equipment essential to the
performing arts at COS. Now we must integrate into our yearly schedule not only our classes and
productions but inspections and repairs to all of our equipment.
5A. Repair of Lighting Postion
Several years ago, our fly system was inspected and the the front of house lighting position in
Kenneth Ford Theater was found to be out of compliance so we have been unable to use it since.
This is an important component of the lighting system in the theater and we have been unable to
adequately light the stage since the inspection.
Please see CQIP 2
Signature Page

learning, program effectiveness and resource allocation.

Neil Carpentier-Alting
